---
title: "SGLT2 Inhibitors vs. Placebo for Heart Failure Hospitalization in Type 2 Diabetes with Established ASCVD"
subtitle: "A reproducible meta-analysis"
author: "Said Adjao"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup

library(tidyverse)
library(meta)

# HF hospitalization, SGLT2i vs placebo. Hazard ratios from 4 CV outcome trials.
sglt2 <- tibble(
  study = c("EMPA-REG OUTCOME", "CANVAS Program", "DECLARE-TIMI 58", "VERTIS-CV"),
  hr    = c(0.65, 0.67, 0.73, 0.70),
  lower = c(0.50, 0.52, 0.61, 0.54),
  upper = c(0.85, 0.87, 0.88, 0.90)
) %>%
  mutate(
    te   = log(hr),                                # log hazard ratio
    sete = (log(upper) - log(lower)) / (2 * 1.96)  # SE on the log scale
  )

m <- metagen(
  TE = te, seTE = sete,
  studlab = study, data = sglt2,
  sm = "HR", common = FALSE, random = TRUE
)
```

## Summary

This report synthesizes SGLT2 inhibitors versus placebo for heart failure
hospitalization in adults with type 2 diabetes and established atherosclerotic
cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). Pooling `r m$k` trials, SGLT2 inhibitors were
associated with a significantly lower risk of HF hospitalization (pooled hazard
ratio **`r round(exp(m$TE.random), 2)`**, 95% CI
`r round(exp(m$lower.random), 2)` to `r round(exp(m$upper.random), 2)`) — an
approximately 30% relative reduction — with no detectable between-study
heterogeneity (I² = `r round(m$I2 * 100, 1)`%).

## Background

Heart failure is a frequent and costly complication of type 2 diabetes, and
hospitalization for heart failure is a key driver of both morbidity and
healthcare expenditure. SGLT2 inhibitors have demonstrated cardiovascular
benefit, but coverage and guideline positioning depend on the strength of the
evidence in specific populations. This analysis synthesizes the randomized
evidence for HF hospitalization in patients with established ASCVD.

## Methods

### Eligibility criteria

Randomized controlled trials were eligible if they enrolled adults with type 2
diabetes and established ASCVD (prior MI, angina, coronary or peripheral
revascularization, ischaemic stroke, or symptomatic PAD), compared an SGLT2
inhibitor against placebo, and reported hospitalization for heart failure.
Trials enrolling primarily on chronic kidney disease or on heart failure were
excluded, to keep the population defined by atherosclerotic disease.

### Information sources and study selection

A PubMed search (RCT filter, 2013–present) returned 250 records. After removal
of duplicates, records were screened in two stages — title/abstract, then full
text — using Rayyan. Secondary analyses and substudies were excluded so that
each trial was represented by a single primary results paper. Four cardiovascular
outcome trials met criteria: EMPA-REG OUTCOME, CANVAS Program, DECLARE-TIMI 58,
and VERTIS-CV.

### Data extraction

Hospitalization for heart failure was extracted as a hazard ratio with 95%
confidence interval from each trial. Hazard ratios were chosen because these are
time-to-event trials with differing follow-up; raw per-arm event counts were not
uniformly reported (CANVAS, for example, reported event rates rather than
counts), making the hazard ratio the consistent and appropriate effect measure
across trials.

### Statistical analysis

Effects were pooled using an inverse-variance random-effects model on the log
scale, implemented with the `meta` package in R. Random effects were chosen a
priori because the trials differed in SGLT2 inhibitor, follow-up duration, and
population composition, making a single common true effect implausible.
Heterogeneity was quantified with I².

## Results

```{r}
#| label: study-table

sglt2 %>%
  mutate(
    HR = round(hr, 2),
    `95% CI` = paste0("(", round(lower, 2), ", ", round(upper, 2), ")")
  ) %>%
  select(Study = study, HR, `95% CI`) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Hazard ratio for HF hospitalization (SGLT2i vs placebo). HR < 1 favors SGLT2i.")
```

```{r}
#| label: forest-plot
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5

forest(m,
       leftcols = c("studlab"),
       label.left = "Favors SGLT2i",
       label.right = "Favors Placebo")
```

Pooling all `r m$k` trials, SGLT2 inhibitors significantly reduced HF
hospitalization (HR **`r round(exp(m$TE.random), 2)`**; 95% CI
`r round(exp(m$lower.random), 2)` to `r round(exp(m$upper.random), 2)`).
Between-study heterogeneity was absent (I² = `r round(m$I2 * 100, 1)`%). Unlike
synthesis of a small or imprecise evidence base, this near-zero heterogeneity
reflects genuine consistency across four large trials with tight confidence
intervals, supporting a robust and reliable effect.

## Limitations

The evidence base comprises four trials; although each is large and the pooled
estimate is precise, four studies remain a modest number. Two trials
(DECLARE-TIMI 58, CANVAS Program) enrolled mixed populations including
primary-prevention participants, so the analysis is not restricted purely to
secondary prevention. Hospitalization for heart failure was a secondary outcome
in most trials. Finally, effect estimates were extracted as reported hazard
ratios rather than re-derived from individual patient data.

## References

1. Zinman B, Wanner C, Lachin JM, et al. Empagliflozin, cardiovascular outcomes, and mortality in type 2 diabetes (EMPA-REG OUTCOME). *N Engl J Med.* 2015;373(22):2117–2128.
2. Neal B, Perkovic V, Mahaffey KW, et al. Canagliflozin and cardiovascular and renal events in type 2 diabetes (CANVAS Program). *N Engl J Med.* 2017;377(7):644–657.
3. Wiviott SD, Raz I, Bonaca MP, et al. Dapagliflozin and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes (DECLARE-TIMI 58). *N Engl J Med.* 2019;380(4):347–357.
4. Cannon CP, Pratley R, Dagogo-Jack S, et al. Cardiovascular outcomes with ertugliflozin in type 2 diabetes (VERTIS-CV). *N Engl J Med.* 2020;383(15):1425–1435.
